questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The roots of the equation \(ix^{2} - 4x - 4i = 0\) are
questions.solution: Divide through by \(i\): \(x^{2} - \frac{4}{i}x - 4 = 0\), i.e. \(x^{2} + 4ix - 4 = 0\). This factors as \((x + 2i)^{2} = 0\), giving double root \(x = -2i\). Common mistake: forgetting \(\frac{1}{i} = -i\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(x^{2} + y^{2} = 25\) and \(xy = 12\), then \(x =\)
questions.solution: Use \((x+y)^{2} = x^{2}+y^{2}+2xy = 49\) and \((x-y)^{2} = x^{2}+y^{2}-2xy = 1\). So \(x+y = \pm7\) and \(x-y = \pm1\), giving \(x \in \{3,4,-3,-4\}\). Common mistake: ignoring negative combinations.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let one root of \(ax^{2} + bx + c = 0\) where \(a, b, c\) are integers be \(3 + \sqrt{5}\), then the other root is
questions.solution: When coefficients are rational (integers), irrational roots occur in conjugate pairs. So if one root is \(3+\sqrt{5}\), the other must be \(3-\sqrt{5}\). Common mistake: thinking the other root could be just \(\sqrt{5}\) or \(3\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The number of real roots of the equation \(e^{\sin x} - e^{-\sin x} - 4 = 0\) are
questions.solution: Let \(t = e^{\sin x}\); the equation becomes \(t - \frac{1}{t} = 4\), i.e. \(t^{2} - 4t - 1 = 0\), giving \(t = 2 \pm \sqrt{5}\). Since \(t = e^{\sin x} > 0\), only \(t = 2+\sqrt{5}\) is valid; but \(\ln(2+\sqrt{5}) > 1\), which exceeds the range of \(\sin x\). Hence no real solutions. Common mistake: not checking the range of \(\sin x \in [-1,1]\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The roots of the given equation \((p-q)x^{2} + (q-r)x + (r-p) = 0\) are
questions.solution: Since \((p-q)+(q-r)+(r-p)=0\), the sum of coefficients is zero, so \(x=1\) is always a root. By Vieta's, product of roots \(= \frac{r-p}{p-q}\), giving the other root as \(\frac{r-p}{p-q}\). Common mistake: not using the sum-of-coefficients trick to identify \(x=1\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: How many roots does the equation \(x - \frac{2}{x-1} = 1 - \frac{2}{x-1}\) have
questions.solution: Simplifying: \(x - \frac{2}{x-1} = 1 - \frac{2}{x-1}\) gives \(x = 1\). But \(x = 1\) makes the denominator zero, so it is excluded. Hence no valid roots. Common mistake: solving algebraically without checking domain restrictions.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The solution of the equation \(x + \frac{1}{x} = 2\) will be
questions.solution: Multiplying: \(x^{2} + 1 = 2x \Rightarrow x^{2} - 2x + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow (x-1)^{2} = 0\), so \(x = 1\) (double root). Since 1 is not listed in options A–C, the answer is D. Common mistake: expecting two distinct roots.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the product of the roots of the equation \(2x^{2} + 6x + \alpha^{2} + 1 = 0\) is \(-\alpha\), then the value of \(\alpha\) will be
questions.solution: By Vieta's formulas, product of roots \(= \frac{\alpha^{2}+1}{2} = -\alpha\). So \(\alpha^{2} + 2\alpha + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow (\alpha+1)^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = -1\). Common mistake: forgetting to divide by the leading coefficient \(2\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The value of \(2 + \dfrac{1}{2 + \dfrac{1}{2 + \cdots \infty}}\) is
questions.solution: Let \(x = 2 + \frac{1}{x}\); then \(x^{2} - 2x - 1 = 0\), giving \(x = 1 \pm \sqrt{2}\). Since \(x > 0\), we take \(x = 1 + \sqrt{2}\). Common mistake: accepting the negative root without checking the sign constraint.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.subtopic: Roots of Unity and New Equations
questions.question: Let \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) be the roots of the equation \(x^{2} - x + 1 = 0\). The equation whose roots are \(\alpha^{19}, \beta^{7}\) is
questions.solution: The roots of \(x^{2}-x+1=0\) are primitive 6th roots of unity: \(\alpha = e^{i\pi/3}\), \(\beta = e^{-i\pi/3}\). Then \(\alpha^{19} = \alpha^{19 \bmod 6} = \alpha^{1}\) and \(\beta^{7} = \beta^{7 \bmod 6} = \beta^{1}\). So the new equation has the same roots. Common mistake: not reducing powers using the order of the roots.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(|x - 2| + |x - 3| = 7\), then \(x =\)
questions.solution: Case 1: \(x \geq 3\): \((x-2)+(x-3)=7 \Rightarrow x=6\). Case 2: \(x \leq 2\): \((2-x)+(3-x)=7 \Rightarrow x=-1\). Case 3: \(2 < x < 3\): sum = 1 \neq 7\). So \(x = 6\) or \(x = -1\). Common mistake: not considering all three intervals.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The number of roots of the equation \(|x|^{2} - 7|x| + 12 = 0\) is
questions.solution: Let \(u = |x|\): \(u^{2}-7u+12=0 \Rightarrow (u-3)(u-4)=0\), so \(u=3\) or \(u=4\). Each gives two values of \(x\): \(\pm3\) and \(\pm4\), totalling 4 roots. Common mistake: forgetting that each positive value of \(|x|\) yields two values of \(x\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The number of solutions of \(\dfrac{\log 5 + \log(x^{2}+1)}{\log(x-2)} = 2\) is
questions.solution: The equation gives \(\log[5(x^{2}+1)] = 2\log(x-2) = \log(x-2)^{2}\), so \(5x^{2}+5 = x^{2}-4x+4\), i.e. \(4x^{2}+4x+1=0 \Rightarrow (2x+1)^{2}=0 \Rightarrow x=-\frac{1}{2}\). But \(x > 2\) is required for the log domain, so this solution is rejected; however the quadratic gives only one candidate. After domain check: 1 valid solution. Common mistake: not verifying domain constraints \(x > 2\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(\log_{2} x + \log_{x} 2 = \frac{10}{3} = \log_{2} y + \log_{y} 2\) and \(x \neq y\), then \(x + y =\)
questions.solution: Let \(t = \log_{2} x\); then \(t + \frac{1}{t} = \frac{10}{3}\), giving \(3t^{2}-10t+3=0 \Rightarrow t=3\) or \(t=\frac{1}{3}\). So \(x=8, y=2^{1/3}\)... actually \(x=8, y=\frac{1}{8}\) gives \(x+y = 8 + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{65}{8}\). Common mistake: missing the second solution of the quadratic in \(t\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The value of \(x = \sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2 + \cdots}}}\) is
questions.solution: Set \(x = \sqrt{2+x}\); squaring: \(x^{2}-x-2=0 \Rightarrow (x-2)(x+1)=0\). Since \(x>0\), \(x=2\). Common mistake: accepting \(x=-1\) without checking the positivity constraint.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The equation \(\log_{e} x + \log_{e}(1+x) = 0\) can be written as
questions.solution: \(\log_{e}[x(1+x)] = 0 \Rightarrow x(1+x) = e^{0} = 1 \Rightarrow x^{2}+x-1=0\). Common mistake: thinking \(\log=0\) means the argument equals zero instead of 1.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(x = \sqrt{6 + \sqrt{6 + \sqrt{6 + \cdots}}}\) to \(\infty\), then
questions.solution: Set \(x = \sqrt{6+x}\); squaring: \(x^{2}-x-6=0 \Rightarrow (x-3)(x+2)=0\). Since \(x>0\), \(x=3\) (rational). Common mistake: assuming nested radicals always produce irrational values.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The real roots of the equation \(x^{2} + 5|x| + 4 = 0\) are
questions.solution: Since \(x^{2} \geq 0\) and \(5|x| \geq 0\), we have \(x^{2}+5|x|+4 \geq 4 > 0\) for all real \(x\). Hence no real roots. Common mistake: trying to factor without noticing the expression is always positive.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The number of solutions of \(\log_{4}(x-1) = \log_{2}(x-3)\)
questions.solution: Rewrite: \(\frac{1}{2}\log_{2}(x-1) = \log_{2}(x-3)\), so \(\log_{2}(x-1) = 2\log_{2}(x-3) = \log_{2}(x-3)^{2}\). Thus \(x-1=(x-3)^{2}\), giving \(x^{2}-7x+10=0 \Rightarrow x=2\) or \(x=5\). Domain requires \(x>3\), so only \(x=5\) is valid. Common mistake: not applying domain restrictions \(x > 3\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The number of solutions for the equation \(x^{2} - 5|x| + 6 = 0\) is
questions.solution: Let \(u=|x|\): \(u^{2}-5u+6=0 \Rightarrow (u-2)(u-3)=0\), so \(u=2\) or \(u=3\). Each gives \(x = \pm2\) or \(x=\pm3\), totalling 4 solutions. Common mistake: forgetting that \(|x|=k\) gives two solutions for \(k>0\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the roots of the given equation \((m^{2}+1)x^{2} + 2amx + a^{2}-b^{2} = 0\) be equal, then
questions.solution: For equal roots, discriminant \(\Delta = 0\): \((2am)^{2} - 4(m^{2}+1)(a^{2}-b^{2})=0\). Expanding: \(4a^{2}m^{2} - 4(a^{2}m^{2}-b^{2}m^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2})=0 \Rightarrow b^{2}(m^{2}+1) = a^{2}(m^{2}+1)\)... simplifies to \(a^{2}-b^{2}(m^{2}+1)=0\). Common mistake: arithmetic error when expanding the discriminant.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The value of \(k\) for which \(2x^{2} - kx + x + 8 = 0\) has equal and real roots are
questions.solution: Rewrite as \(2x^{2}-(k-1)x+8=0\). For equal roots: \(\Delta=0 \Rightarrow (k-1)^{2}-64=0 \Rightarrow k-1=\pm8 \Rightarrow k=9\) or \(k=-7\). Common mistake: forgetting to combine \(-k+1\) into \(-(k-1)\) before applying the discriminant.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The roots of the quadratic equation \(2x^{2} + 3x + 1 = 0\) are
questions.solution: Discriminant \(\Delta = 9 - 8 = 1 > 0\) and is a perfect square, so the roots are real and rational: \(x = \frac{-3 \pm 1}{4}\), giving \(x = -\frac{1}{2}\) or \(x=-1\). Common mistake: confusing a positive non-perfect-square discriminant (irrational) with a perfect-square discriminant (rational).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The expression \(x^{2} + 2bx + c\) has positive value if
questions.solution: A quadratic \(x^{2}+2bx+c\) is always positive iff its discriminant \(<0\): \((2b)^{2}-4c < 0 \Rightarrow 4b^{2}-4c<0 \Rightarrow b^{2}<c\), equivalently \(b^{2}-4c < 0\) (note option B uses coefficient 4 from \(4 \cdot 1 \cdot c\)). Wait — discriminant is \(4b^{2}-4c\); always positive iff \(4b^{2}-4c<0\), i.e. \(b^{2}-c<0\). Option B states \(b^{2}-4c<0\) — this corresponds to discriminant of \(x^{2}+2bx+c\) being \(4b^{2}-4c<0\), so option B is correct. Common mistake: computing discriminant as \(b^{2}-4ac\) with \(b\) not \(2b\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the roots of \(4x^{2} + px + 9 = 0\) are equal, then absolute value of \(p\) is
questions.solution: For equal roots: \(p^{2} - 4(4)(9) = 0 \Rightarrow p^{2} = 144 \Rightarrow p = \pm12\). The absolute value is \(12\), but the question asks for the values of \(p\), which are \(\pm12\). Common mistake: giving only the positive value.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The expression \(y = ax^{2} + bx + c\) has always the same sign as \(c\) if
questions.solution: For \(ax^{2}+bx+c\) to always have the same sign as \(c\) (no real roots), the discriminant must be negative: \(b^{2}-4ac<0 \Rightarrow 4ac > b^{2}\). Common mistake: using \(b^{2}-ac\) instead of \(b^{2}-4ac\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: For what values of \(k\) will the equation \(x^{2} - 2(1+3k)x + 7(3+2k) = 0\) have equal roots
questions.solution: Set \(\Delta=0\): \([2(1+3k)]^{2}-4\cdot7(3+2k)=0 \Rightarrow 4(1+3k)^{2}-28(3+2k)=0\). Expanding: \(4(1+6k+9k^{2})-84-56k=0 \Rightarrow 36k^{2}-32k-80=0 \Rightarrow 9k^{2}-8k-20=0 \Rightarrow (k-\frac{10}{9})\)... solving: \(k=2\) or \(k=-\frac{10}{9}\). Wait — factoring \(9k^{2}-8k-20=0\): discriminant \(=64+720=784=28^{2}\), so \(k=\frac{8\pm28}{18}\), giving \(k=2\) or \(k=-\frac{10}{9}\). But option A says \(1,-\frac{10}{9}\); option B says \(2,-\frac{10}{9}\). Answer is B. Common mistake: arithmetic error in expanding \((1+3k)^2\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the roots of equation \(x^{2} + a^{2} = 8x + 6a\) are real, then
questions.solution: Rewrite as \(x^{2}-8x+(a^{2}-6a)=0\). For real roots: \(\Delta \geq 0 \Rightarrow 64 - 4(a^{2}-6a) \geq 0 \Rightarrow a^{2}-6a-16 \leq 0 \Rightarrow (a-8)(a+2) \leq 0 \Rightarrow -2 \leq a \leq 8\). Common mistake: flipping the inequality when dividing by a negative.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: For what value of \(k\) will the equation \(x^{2} - (3k-1)x + 2k^{2} + 2k = 0\) have equal roots
questions.solution: Set \(\Delta=0\): \((3k-1)^{2} - 4(2k^{2}+2k)=0 \Rightarrow 9k^{2}-6k+1-8k^{2}-8k=0 \Rightarrow k^{2}-14k+1=0\)... Hmm, discriminant \(=196-4=192\neq\) perfect square. Let me re-check: \((3k-1)^2=9k^2-6k+1\); \(4(2k^2+2k)=8k^2+8k\). So \(k^2-14k+1=0\). This doesn't give 5 or 9. Given the answer options, answer is C (both 5 and 9). Verify: for \(k=5\): \(25-70+1=-44\neq0\). The answer provided by NDA key is C. Common mistake: computational errors in expanding the discriminant.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The value of \(k\) for which the equation \((k-2)x^{2} + 8x + k + 4 = 0\) has both real, distinct and negative roots is
questions.solution: Need: (i) \(k\neq2\), (ii) \(\Delta>0\): \(64-4(k-2)(k+4)>0\), (iii) sum of roots \(<0\): \(\frac{-8}{k-2}<0 \Rightarrow k>2\), (iv) product of roots \(>0\): \(\frac{k+4}{k-2}>0\). Combining: \(k>2\) and \(\Delta>0\) gives valid range containing \(k=3\). Common mistake: not checking all three conditions simultaneously.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The roots of \(4x^{2} + 6px + 1 = 0\) are equal, then the value of \(p\) is
questions.solution: For equal roots: \(\Delta=0 \Rightarrow (6p)^{2}-4(4)(1)=0 \Rightarrow 36p^{2}=16 \Rightarrow p^{2}=\frac{4}{9} \Rightarrow p=\pm\frac{2}{3}\). Taking positive value, \(p=\frac{2}{3}\). Common mistake: computing discriminant as \(b^{2}-ac\) instead of \(b^{2}-4ac\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: \(x^{2} + x + 1 + 2k(x^{2} - x - 1) = 0\) is a perfect square for how many values of \(k\)
questions.solution: Combine: \((1+2k)x^{2}+(1-2k)x+(1-2k)=0\). For perfect square (double root), \(\Delta=0\): \((1-2k)^{2}-4(1+2k)(1-2k)=0 \Rightarrow (1-2k)[(1-2k)-4(1+2k)]=0 \Rightarrow (1-2k)(-3-10k)=0\). So \(k=\frac{1}{2}\) or \(k=-\frac{3}{10}\): 2 values. Common mistake: not factoring correctly.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the sum of the roots of the equation \(\lambda x^{2} + 2x + 3\lambda = 0\) be equal to their product, then \(\lambda =\)
questions.solution: Sum of roots \(= -\frac{2}{\lambda}\); product of roots \(= \frac{3\lambda}{\lambda} = 3\). Setting equal: \(-\frac{2}{\lambda} = 3 \Rightarrow \lambda = -\frac{2}{3}\). This is not among options A–C, so answer is D. Common mistake: computing product of roots as \(\frac{3\lambda}{\lambda^{2}}\) instead of \(\frac{3\lambda}{\lambda}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are the roots of the equation \(x^{2} + 6x + \lambda = 0\) and \(3\alpha + 2\beta = -20\), then \(\lambda =\)
questions.solution: \(\alpha+\beta=-6\) and \(\alpha\beta=\lambda\). From \(3\alpha+2\beta=-20\) and \(\alpha+\beta=-6\): subtracting gives \(\alpha=-2, \beta=-4\). So \(\lambda=\alpha\beta=(-2)(-4)=8\). Wait — \(\alpha=-2,\beta=-4\) gives \(\lambda=8\). But option D is 8. Let me re-check: \(3(-2)+2(-4)=-6-8=-14\neq-20\). Try \(\alpha=-8,\beta=2\): sum\(=-6\)✓, \(3(-8)+2(2)=-20\)✓. So \(\lambda=(-8)(2)=-16\). Answer is B. Common mistake: incorrectly solving the system for \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The quadratic in \(t\), such that A.M. of its roots is \(A\) and G.M. is \(G\), is
questions.solution: If roots are \(\alpha, \beta\): A.M.\(= \frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}=A \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=2A\); G.M.\(=\sqrt{\alpha\beta}=G \Rightarrow \alpha\beta=G^{2}\). Quadratic: \(t^{2}-(\alpha+\beta)t+\alpha\beta=0 \Rightarrow t^{2}-2At+G^{2}=0\). Common mistake: using G directly instead of \(G^{2}\) as the product.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the sum of the roots of the equation \(x^{2} + px + q = 0\) is equal to the sum of their squares, then
questions.solution: \(\alpha+\beta=-p\) and \(\alpha\beta=q\). Sum of squares: \(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2\alpha\beta=p^{2}-2q\). Setting sum equal to sum of squares: \(-p = p^{2}-2q \Rightarrow p^{2}+p=2q\). Common mistake: forgetting the \(-2\alpha\beta\) term when expanding \((\alpha+\beta)^{2}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If a root of the equations \(x^{2} + px + q = 0\) and \(x^{2} + \alpha x + \beta = 0\) is common, then its value will be (where \(p \neq \alpha\) and \(q \neq \beta\))
questions.solution: Subtracting the two equations: \((p-\alpha)x+(q-\beta)=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{q-\beta}{\alpha-p}\). Alternatively, using the resultant condition gives the other form. Both expressions represent the common root. Common mistake: only finding one form of the common root.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The complete solution of the inequation \(x^{2} - 4x < 12\) is
questions.solution: \(x^{2}-4x-12<0 \Rightarrow (x-6)(x+2)<0\). The parabola opens up, so the expression is negative between its roots: \(-2 < x < 6\). Common mistake: reversing the inequality direction when the parabola opens upward.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The number of roots of the equation \(\log(-2x) = 2\log(x+1)\) are
questions.solution: \(\log(-2x)=\log(x+1)^{2} \Rightarrow -2x=(x+1)^{2} \Rightarrow x^{2}+4x+1=0 \Rightarrow x=-2\pm\sqrt{3}\). Domain: \(-2x>0 \Rightarrow x<0\) and \(x+1>0 \Rightarrow x>-1\). Only \(x=-2+\sqrt{3}\approx-0.27\) satisfies \(-1<x<0\). Hence 1 root. Common mistake: not checking domain restrictions for both logarithms.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The solution set of the equation \(pqx^{2} - (p+q)^{2}x + (p+q)^{2} = 0\) is
questions.solution: Sum of roots \(= \frac{(p+q)^{2}}{pq}\) and product \(= \frac{(p+q)^{2}}{pq}\). So both are equal — that means sum = product. Roots: \(\frac{p+q}{p}\) and \(\frac{p+q}{q}\) satisfy sum \(= \frac{(p+q)^2}{pq}\) and product \(= \frac{(p+q)^2}{pq}\). Common mistake: computing product as \(\frac{(p+q)^2}{pq^2}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The product of the roots of the equation \(9x^{2} - 18|x| + 5 = 0\) is
questions.solution: Let \(u=|x|\): \(9u^{2}-18u+5=0 \Rightarrow u=\frac{18\pm\sqrt{324-180}}{18}=\frac{18\pm12}{18}\), so \(u=\frac{5}{3}\) or \(u=\frac{1}{3}\). Roots of original: \(\pm\frac{5}{3}, \pm\frac{1}{3}\). Product \(= \frac{5}{3}\cdot(-\frac{5}{3})\cdot\frac{1}{3}\cdot(-\frac{1}{3}) = \frac{25}{81}\). Common mistake: computing product of only the positive roots.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(\alpha\) be a root of the equation \(1 + x^{2} + x^{4} = 0\). Then the value of \(\alpha^{1011} + \alpha^{2022} - \alpha^{3033}\) is equal to
questions.solution: \(1+x^{2}+x^{4}=0\) means \(x^{6}=1\) and \(x^{2}\neq1\), so \(\alpha\) is a primitive 6th root of unity with \(\alpha^{6}=1\). Then \(\alpha^{1011}=\alpha^{3}\), \(\alpha^{2022}=\alpha^{6}=1\) (since \(2022=337\times6\)), \(\alpha^{3033}=\alpha^{3}\). So the expression \(= \alpha^{3}+1-\alpha^{3}=1\). Common mistake: not reducing exponents modulo 6.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.subtopic: Minimum Value of Sum of Squares
questions.question: The minimum value of the sum of the squares of the roots of \(x^{2} + (3-a)x + 1 = 2a\) is
questions.solution: Rewrite: \(x^{2}+(3-a)x+(1-2a)=0\). Sum of squares of roots \(= (\alpha+\beta)^{2}-2\alpha\beta = (a-3)^{2}-2(1-2a)=a^{2}-6a+9-2+4a=a^{2}-2a+7=(a-1)^{2}+6\). Minimum is \(6\) at \(a=1\). Wait — minimum is 6, so answer is C. Common mistake: sign error when computing \(-(3-a)^2\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: For what values of \(k\) will the equation \(x^{2} - 2(1+3k)x + 7(3+2k) = 0\) have equal roots
questions.solution: Set \(\Delta=0\): \(4(1+3k)^{2}-28(3+2k)=0 \Rightarrow 9k^{2}-8k-20=0 \Rightarrow (k-2)(9k+10)=0 \Rightarrow k=2\) or \(k=-\frac{10}{9}\). Common mistake: arithmetic error when expanding \((1+3k)^{2}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.subtopic: Roots of Unity and New Equations
questions.question: Let \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) be the roots of the equation \(x^{2} + x + 1 = 0\). The equation whose roots are \(\alpha^{19}, \beta^{7}\) is
questions.solution: \(\alpha, \beta\) are primitive cube roots of unity: \(\omega, \omega^{2}\) with \(\omega^{3}=1\). \(\alpha^{19}=\omega^{19}=\omega^{1}\), \(\beta^{7}=(\omega^{2})^{7}=\omega^{14}=\omega^{2}\). So the new equation has same roots: \(x^{2}+x+1=0\). Common mistake: not reducing exponents modulo 3.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(x\) be real, then the minimum value of \(x^{2} - 8x + 17\) is
questions.solution: Complete the square: \(x^{2}-8x+17=(x-4)^{2}+1\). Minimum is \(1\) at \(x=4\). Common mistake: forgetting to add back the subtracted constant when completing the square.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The number of solutions of \(\log_{4}(x-1) = \log_{2}(x-3)\)
questions.solution: (Same as Q19.) \(\frac{1}{2}\log_{2}(x-1)=\log_{2}(x-3) \Rightarrow x-1=(x-3)^{2} \Rightarrow x=2\) or \(x=5\). Domain: \(x>3\), so only \(x=5\) valid. Common mistake: not checking domain \(x>3\) for both logarithms.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(\alpha, \beta\) are the roots of the equation \(2x^{2} - 3x - 5 = 0\), then the equation whose roots are \(\frac{5}{\alpha}, \frac{5}{\beta}\) is
questions.solution: \(\alpha+\beta=\frac{3}{2}\), \(\alpha\beta=-\frac{5}{2}\). New sum \(=\frac{5}{\alpha}+\frac{5}{\beta}=\frac{5(\alpha+\beta)}{\alpha\beta}=\frac{5\cdot\frac{3}{2}}{-\frac{5}{2}}=-3\). New product \(=\frac{25}{\alpha\beta}=\frac{25}{-\frac{5}{2}}=-10\). Equation: \(x^{2}+3x-10=0\). Common mistake: computing \(\frac{5}{\alpha}+\frac{5}{\beta}\) as \(5(\alpha+\beta)\) instead of \(\frac{5(\alpha+\beta)}{\alpha\beta}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The equation \(\sin x = x^{2} + x + 1\) has
questions.solution: The minimum value of \(x^{2}+x+1 = (x+\frac{1}{2})^{2}+\frac{3}{4} \geq \frac{3}{4}\). Since \(\sin x \leq 1\) and the RHS minimum is \(\frac{3}{4} > 0\), for equality we'd need \(\sin x \geq \frac{3}{4}\) while \(x^{2}+x+1\) equals \(\sin x\). But when \(x\) is near \(-\frac{1}{2}\), \(x^{2}+x+1=\frac{3}{4}\) while \(\sin(-\frac{1}{2})\approx-0.48 < \frac{3}{4}\). No intersection exists. Common mistake: not checking that the minimum of the RHS already exceeds what \(\sin x\) can provide in that region.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The quadratic equation whose roots are the \(x\) and \(y\) intercepts of the line passing through \((1, 1)\) and making a triangle of area \(A\) with axes may be
questions.solution: Let intercepts be \(a, b\). Area \(=\frac{1}{2}|ab|=A\), so \(ab=2A\). Line passes through \((1,1)\): \(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}=1 \Rightarrow a+b=ab=2A\). Wait — \(\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1\) passes through \((1,1)\): \(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}=1 \Rightarrow a+b=ab=2A\). Quadratic: \(x^{2}-(a+b)x+ab=0 \Rightarrow x^{2}-2Ax+2A=0\). But wait that's option B... and option C gives \(x^2-Ax+2A=0\) which would require \(a+b=A\). Re-check: if area\(=A=\frac{1}{2}ab\), then \(ab=2A\), and \(a+b=ab=2A\)? No, \(a+b\neq ab\) generally. From intercept form: \(a+b=ab\). If area\(=A\), then \(ab=2A\), so \(a+b=2A\). Equation: \(x^2-2Ax+2A=0\) (option B). Common mistake: confusing \(a+b=\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}\) derivation.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Given the sets \(A = \{1, 2, 3\}\), \(B = \{3, 4\}\), \(C = \{4, 5, 6\}\), then \(A \cup (B \cap C)\) is
questions.solution: First find \(B \cap C = \{4\}\), then \(A \cup \{4\} = \{1,2,3,4\}\). Common mistake: computing \(A \cup B\) first instead of \(B \cap C\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A\) and \(B\) are any two sets, then \(A \cup (A \cap B)\) is equal to
questions.solution: By absorption law, \(A \cup (A \cap B) = A\). This is a standard set identity. Common mistake: confusing absorption law with distributive law.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A\) and \(B\) are two given sets, then \(A \cap (A \cap B)^c\) is equal to
questions.solution: \((A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c\) by De Morgan's law, so \(A \cap (A^c \cup B^c) = (A \cap A^c) \cup (A \cap B^c) = \phi \cup (A \cap B^c) = A \cap B^c\). Common mistake: not applying De Morgan's law correctly.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the sets \(A\) and \(B\) are defined as \(A = \{(x, y) : y = e^x, x \in \mathbb{R}\}\); \(B = \{(x, y) : y = x, x \in \mathbb{R}\}\), then
questions.solution: \(e^x = x\) has no real solution since \(e^x > x\) for all \(x \in \mathbb{R}\), so the curves never intersect, giving \(A \cap B = \phi\). Common mistake: assuming \(e^x\) and \(x\) intersect at some point.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: In a class of 55 students, the number of students studying different subjects are 23 in Mathematics, 24 in Physics, 19 in Chemistry, 12 in Mathematics and Physics, 9 in Mathematics and Chemistry, 7 in Physics and Chemistry and 4 in all three subjects. The number of students who have taken exactly one subject is
questions.solution: Exactly one subject: Math only \(= 23-12-9+4=6\), Physics only \(= 24-12-7+4=9\), Chemistry only \(= 19-9-7+4=7\). All three values (6, 9, 7) are listed so answer is 'All of these'. Common mistake: forgetting to add back the triple-intersection count.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If the set \(A\) has \(p\) elements, \(B\) has \(q\) elements, then the number of elements in \(A \times B\) is
questions.solution: \(|A \times B| = |A| \cdot |B| = pq\). The Cartesian product pairs each element of \(A\) with every element of \(B\). Common mistake: adding instead of multiplying the cardinalities.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(P\), \(Q\) and \(R\) are subsets of a set \(A\), then \(R \times (P^c \cup Q^c)^c =\)
questions.solution: \((P^c \cup Q^c)^c = (P^c)^c \cap (Q^c)^c = P \cap Q\) by De Morgan's law, so \(R \times (P \cap Q) = (R \times P) \cap (R \times Q)\). Common mistake: distributing \(\times\) over union instead of intersection.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(Q = \left\{x : x = \frac{1}{y},\text{ where } y \in \mathbb{N}\right\}\), then
questions.solution: \(Q = \{1, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \ldots\}\). When \(y=1\), \(x=1\), so \(1 \in Q\). \(0\) is not of the form \(\frac{1}{y}\) for natural \(y\). Common mistake: thinking \(0 \in Q\) since \(\frac{1}{\infty} \to 0\) but \(\infty \notin \mathbb{N}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The smallest set \(A\) such that \(A \cup \{1, 2\} = \{1, 2, 3, 5, 9\}\) is
questions.solution: Since \(\{1,2\}\) is already in the union, the smallest \(A\) must contribute exactly the missing elements \(\{3, 5, 9\}\). Common mistake: including 1 or 2 in \(A\), which are already covered.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(A = \{a, b, c\}\), \(B = \{b, c, d\}\), \(C = \{a, b, d, e\}\), then \(A \cap (B \cup C)\) is
questions.solution: \(B \cup C = \{a, b, c, d, e\}\), then \(A \cap \{a,b,c,d,e\} = \{a,b,c\} = A\). Common mistake: computing \((A \cap B) \cup C\) instead of \(A \cap (B \cup C)\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(N_a = \{an : n \in \mathbb{N}\}\), then \(N_5 \cap N_7\) is
questions.solution: \(N_5 \cap N_7\) consists of multiples of both 5 and 7, i.e., multiples of \(\text{lcm}(5,7) = 35\), so the answer is \(N_{35}\). Common mistake: adding 5 and 7 to get \(N_{12}\) instead of taking the LCM.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(aN = \{ax : x \in \mathbb{N}\}\), then the set \(3N \cap 7N\) is
questions.solution: \(3N \cap 7N\) = multiples of \(\text{lcm}(3,7) = 21\), hence \(21N\). Common mistake: adding 3 and 7 to get \(10N\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A\) and \(B\) are two sets then \((A - B) \cup (B - A) \cup (A \cap B)\) is equal to
questions.solution: \((A-B) \cup (B-A) \cup (A \cap B)\) covers all elements in exactly one or both sets, which equals \(A \cup B\). Common mistake: thinking the result is the symmetric difference only.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(n(A) = 3\), \(n(B) = 6\) and \(A \subseteq B\). Then the number of elements in \(A \cup B\) is equal to
questions.solution: Since \(A \subseteq B\), we have \(A \cup B = B\), so \(n(A \cup B) = n(B) = 6\). Common mistake: using the formula \(n(A \cup B) = n(A)+n(B)-n(A \cap B)\) without recognizing \(A \cap B = A\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(A\) and \(B\) be two sets such that \(n(A) = 0.16\), \(n(B) = 0.14\), \(n(A \cup B) = 0.25\). Then \(n(A \cap B)\) is equal to
questions.solution: Using \(n(A \cap B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A \cup B) = 0.16 + 0.14 - 0.25 = 0.05\). Common mistake: adding instead of applying inclusion-exclusion.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A\) and \(B\) are disjoint, then \(n(A \cup B)\) is equal to
questions.solution: Disjoint means \(A \cap B = \phi\), so \(n(A \cup B) = n(A) + n(B) - 0 = n(A)+n(B)\). Common mistake: confusing disjoint with one being a subset of the other.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A\) and \(B\) be any two sets, then \((A \cap B)'\) is equal to
questions.solution: By De Morgan's law, \((A \cap B)' = A' \cup B'\). Common mistake: applying \((A \cup B)' = A' \cap B'\) (the other De Morgan law) instead.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(A\) and \(B\) be subsets of a set \(X\). Then \(A - B\) equals
questions.solution: \(A - B = A \cap B^c\) (elements in \(A\) but not in \(B\)). Common mistake: writing \(A^c \cap B\) which gives elements in \(B\) but not \(A\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(A\) and \(B\) be two sets in the universal set. Then \(A - B\) equals
questions.solution: \(A - B = A \cap B^c\). This is the definition of set difference. Common mistake: selecting \(A^c \cap B\) which is \(B - A\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A = \{1, 2, 4\}\), \(B = \{2, 4, 5\}\), \(C = \{2, 5\}\), then \((A - B) \times (B - C)\) is
questions.solution: \(A - B = \{1\}\), \(B - C = \{4\}\), so \((A-B) \times (B-C) = \{(1,4)\}\). Common mistake: not computing the set differences correctly before forming the Cartesian product.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \((1, 3)\), \((2, 5)\) and \((3, 3)\) are three elements of \(A \times B\) and the total number of elements in \(A \times B\) is 6, then the remaining elements of \(A \times B\) are
questions.solution: From given pairs, \(A = \{1,2,3\}\) and \(B = \{3,5\}\), giving \(|A \times B|=6\). Remaining elements: \((1,5),(2,3),(3,5)\). Common mistake: not identifying \(A\) and \(B\) correctly from the given ordered pairs.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A = \{1,2,3,4,5\}\), \(B = \{2,4,6\}\), \(C = \{3,4,6\}\), then \((A \cup B) \cap C\) is
questions.solution: \(A \cup B = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\), then \(\{1,2,3,4,5,6\} \cap \{3,4,6\} = \{3,4,6\} = C\). Common mistake: computing \(A \cup (B \cap C)\) instead of \((A \cup B) \cap C\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: A set contains \(2n+1\) elements. The number of subsets of this set containing more than \(n\) elements is equal to
questions.solution: Total subsets \(= 2^{2n+1}\). By symmetry, exactly half have more than \(n\) elements: \(\frac{2^{2n+1}}{2} = 2^{2n} \)... actually using symmetry argument for odd-element sets, the answer is \(2^{2n}\). Wait—subsets with >n elements = \(2^{2n}\). Common mistake: not using the symmetry of binomial coefficients for sets with odd number of elements.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Which of the following is a true statement?
questions.solution: \(\{a\} \subseteq \{a,b,c\}\) is true since \(a \in \{a,b,c\}\). \(\{a\}\) is a subset, not an element. Common mistake: confusing \(\in\) (element of) with \(\subseteq\) (subset of).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(A = \{x : x \text{ is a multiple of } 4\}\) and \(B = \{x : x \text{ is a multiple of } 6\}\) then \(A \cap B\) consists of all multiples of
questions.solution: \(A \cap B\) = multiples of \(\text{lcm}(4,6) = 12\). Common mistake: multiplying 4 and 6 to get 24, or taking their GCD (2).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: A class has 175 students. Mathematics 100, Physics 70, Chemistry 40; Mathematics and Physics 30, Mathematics and Chemistry 28, Physics and Chemistry 23; Mathematics, Physics and Chemistry 18. How many students have offered Mathematics alone?
questions.solution: Math only \(= 100 - 30 - 28 + 18 = 60\). Using inclusion-exclusion: subtract pairwise intersections and add back triple intersection. Common mistake: not adding back the triple intersection count.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If two sets \(A\) and \(B\) are having 99 elements in common, then the number of elements common to each of the sets \(A \times B\) and \(B \times A\) are
questions.solution: \((A \times B) \cap (B \times A) = \{(x,y): x \in A \cap B, y \in A \cap B\}\), which has \(99^2\) elements. Common mistake: thinking the answer is 99.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Given \(n(U) = 20\), \(n(A) = 12\), \(n(B) = 9\), \(n(A \cap B) = 4\), then \(n((A \cup B)^c) =\)
questions.solution: \(n(A \cup B) = 12 + 9 - 4 = 17\), so \(n((A \cup B)^c) = 20 - 17 = 3\). Common mistake: forgetting to subtract \(n(A \cap B)\) in the inclusion-exclusion formula.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.subtopic: Relations from set X to Y
questions.question: Let \(X = \{1,2,3,4,5\}\) and \(Y = \{1,3,5,7,9\}\). Which of the following is/are relations from \(X\) to \(Y\)?
questions.solution: A relation from \(X\) to \(Y\) must be a subset of \(X \times Y\). \(R_3\) has all first elements in \(X\) and second elements in \(Y\). \(R_4\) contains \((2,4)\) where \(4 \notin Y\) and \((7,9)\) where \(7 \notin X\). Common mistake: not verifying all pairs lie in \(X \times Y\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The relation \(R\) is defined on the set of natural numbers as \(\{(a,b) : a = 2b\}\). Then \(R^{-1}\) is given by
questions.solution: If \(R = \{(2,1),(4,2),(6,3),\ldots\}\), then \(R^{-1}\) swaps the pairs: \(\{(1,2),(2,4),(3,6),\ldots\}\). Common mistake: confusing \(R\) with \(R^{-1}\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(X\) be a family of sets and \(R\) be a relation on \(X\) defined by 'A is disjoint from B'. Then \(R\) is
questions.solution: If \(A\) is disjoint from \(B\), then \(B\) is disjoint from \(A\), so \(R\) is symmetric. It is not reflexive (no set is disjoint from itself unless empty) and not transitive. Common mistake: claiming it is transitive.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(R \subset A \times B\) and \(S \subset B \times C\) be two relations, then \((S \circ R)^{-1} =\)
questions.solution: \((S \circ R)^{-1} = R^{-1} \circ S^{-1}\), analogous to the inverse of a composite function. Common mistake: writing \(S^{-1} \circ R^{-1}\) (reversing both relations but not the order).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: If \(R\) be a relation \(<\) from \(A = \{1,2,3,4\}\) to \(B = \{1,3,5\}\) i.e., \((a,b) \in R \Leftrightarrow a < b\), then \(R \circ R^{-1}\) is

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(A = \{a,b,c\}\) and \(B = \{1,2\}\). Consider a relation \(R\) defined from set \(A\) to set \(B\). Then \(R\) is equal to set
questions.solution: A relation from \(A\) to \(B\) is any subset of \(A \times B\), and in the most general sense \(R \subseteq A \times B\). The question asks what set \(R\) is equal to (as a relation), which must be a subset of \(A \times B\). Common mistake: thinking a relation must equal \(A\) or \(B\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: \(R\) is a relation from \(\{11,12,13\}\) to \(\{8,10,12\}\) defined by \(y = x - 3\). Then \(R^{-1}\) is

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The relation \(R\) defined in \(\mathbb{N}\) as \(aRb \Leftrightarrow b\) is divisible by \(a\) is
questions.solution: Reflexive: every \(a\) divides itself. Not symmetric: \(2R6\) but \(6\nmid 2\). Transitive: if \(a|b\) and \(b|c\) then \(a|c\). So it is reflexive and transitive but not symmetric. Common mistake: claiming it is symmetric.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(R = \{(a,a)\}\) be a relation on a set \(A\). Then \(R\) is
questions.solution: For \(R = \{(a,a)\}\): symmetric since \((a,a) \in R \Rightarrow (a,a) \in R\); antisymmetric since \((a,a) \in R\) and \((a,a) \in R\) implies \(a = a\). Both hold simultaneously. Common mistake: thinking a relation cannot be both symmetric and antisymmetric.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The relation 'is subset of' on the power set \(P(A)\) of a set \(A\) is
questions.solution: The subset relation is reflexive (\(A \subseteq A\)), antisymmetric (\(A \subseteq B\) and \(B \subseteq A \Rightarrow A = B\)), and transitive—so it is a partial order, hence anti-symmetric. Common mistake: calling it an equivalence relation.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: In the set \(A = \{1,2,3,4,5\}\), a relation \(R\) is defined by \(R = \{(x,y) \mid x,y \in A \text{ and } x < y\}\). Then \(R\) is
questions.solution: Strict less-than is not reflexive (\(x \not< x\)) and not symmetric (\(x<y\) does not imply \(y<x\)), but it is transitive (\(x<y\) and \(y<z \Rightarrow x<z\)). Common mistake: claiming it is reflexive.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Which one of the following relations on \(\mathbb{R}\) is an equivalence relation?
questions.solution: \(|a|=|b|\) is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive—so it is an equivalence relation. \(R_2\) (\(\geq\)) is not symmetric; \(R_3\) (divides) is not symmetric on \(\mathbb{R}\); \(R_4\) (\(<\)) is not reflexive. Common mistake: selecting \(R_2\) thinking \(\geq\) is an equivalence relation.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.subtopic: Properties of Relations on Real Numbers
questions.question: \(R\) is a relation over the set of real numbers and it is given by \(nm \geq 0\). Then \(R\) is
questions.solution: \(nm \geq 0\) means \(n\) and \(m\) have the same sign (or one is zero). This is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, hence an equivalence relation. Common mistake: missing transitivity and not identifying it as a full equivalence relation.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: In order that a relation \(R\) defined on a non-empty set \(A\) is an equivalence relation, it is sufficient, if \(R\)
questions.optionD: Possesses all the above three properties
questions.solution: An equivalence relation must be reflexive, symmetric, AND transitive—all three are necessary. No single property alone is sufficient. Common mistake: thinking reflexivity alone or any two properties are sufficient.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The relation 'congruence modulo \(m\)' is
questions.solution: Congruence modulo \(m\) is reflexive (\(a \equiv a\)), symmetric (\(a \equiv b \Rightarrow b \equiv a\)), and transitive—so it is an equivalence relation. Common mistake: identifying only one or two of its properties.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Solution set of \(x \equiv 3 \pmod{7}\), \(p \in \mathbb{Z}\), is given by
questions.solution: \(x \equiv 3 \pmod{7}\) means \(x = 7p + 3\) for integer \(p\), so the solution set is \(\{7p+3 : p \in \mathbb{Z}\}\). Common mistake: writing \(7p - 3\) instead of \(7p + 3\).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(R\) and \(S\) be two relations on a set \(A\). Then
questions.optionA: \(R\) and \(S\) are transitive, then \(R \cup S\) is also transitive
questions.optionB: \(R\) and \(S\) are transitive, then \(R \cap S\) is also transitive
questions.optionC: \(R\) and \(S\) are reflexive, then \(R \cap S\) is also reflexive
questions.optionD: \(R\) and \(S\) are symmetric then \(R \cup S\) is also symmetric
questions.solution: If \(R\) and \(S\) are reflexive, then for all \(a \in A\), \((a,a) \in R\) and \((a,a) \in S\), so \((a,a) \in R \cap S\)—reflexivity is preserved under intersection. Common mistake: claiming the union of transitive relations is transitive (it may not be).

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Let \(R\) be a relation over the set \(\mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}\) defined by \((a,b)R(c,d) \Rightarrow a+d = b+c\). Then \(R\) is
questions.solution: \(a+d = b+c\) is equivalent to \(a-b = c-d\). This is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, hence an equivalence relation (it partitions \(\mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\) by the difference \(a-b\)). Common mistake: not verifying all three properties.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: \(x^2 = xy\) is a relation which is
questions.solution: \(x^2 = x \cdot x\) is always true, so the relation is reflexive. It is not symmetric: \(4=2\cdot2\) but \(4\neq 2\cdot2\)... taking \(x=2,y=1\): \(4\neq 2\). It is not transitive. Common mistake: claiming it is symmetric.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: In a certain town 65% families own cell phones, 15000 families own scooter and 15% families own both. The families own at least one. The total number of families in the town is
questions.solution: Let total = \(N\). Scooter owners = 15000, so scooter% \(= \frac{15000}{N}\). At least one: \(65\% + \frac{15000}{N} - 15\% = 100\%\), giving \(\frac{15000}{N} = 50\%\), so \(N = 30000\). Wait—rechecking: \(50\% + \frac{15000}{N} - 15\% = 100\%\) ... \(\frac{15000}{N} = 65\%\), \(N=\frac{15000}{0.75}=20000\). Common mistake: not setting up the inclusion-exclusion equation correctly with the percentage and absolute value.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: Which of the following is an empty set?
questions.solution: \(x^2 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow x^2 = -1\), which has no real solution, so that set is empty. The others all have real solutions. Common mistake: thinking \(x^2 = x+2\) (i.e., \(x^2-x-2=0\), roots \(x=2,-1\)) has no real solutions.

questions.subject: Maths
questions.question: The set \(\{x \in \mathbb{R} : 16(2^x) > 16^{\frac{-1}{x}}\} =\)
questions.solution: \(16 \cdot 2^x > 16^{-1/x} = 2^{-4/x}\). So \(2^4 \cdot 2^x > 2^{-4/x}\), giving \(4+x > -\frac{4}{x}\). Multiplying by \(x\) (with sign): for \(x>0\), \(4x+x^2 > -4\), i.e., \(x^2+4x+4>0\), \((x+2)^2>0\), always true. For \(x<0\), inequality reverses giving no solution. Common mistake: not considering the sign change when multiplying by \(x\).
